- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `side-effecting GET`

**Mechanism**: The `unsubscribe` action is routed via `GET` (`config/routes.rb` lines 444–445) and performs a write operation (`tu.save!`) that changes the user's notification level. HTTP GET is defined as idempotent and safe; this violates that contract. Email security scanners and link-prefetch agents that follow every link in an email will trigger an unsubscribe without user intent.

**Consequence**: A user's notification preferences can be silently toggled to `muted` or `regular` by any agent that prefetches or scans the unsubscribe URL in the email. The user never intentionally clicked unsubscribe but their notification level is permanently changed.

**Evidence**: `config/routes.rb` diff lines 444–445: `get "t/:slug/:topic_id/unsubscribe" => "topics#unsubscribe"` and `get "t/:topic_id/unsubscribe" => "topics#unsubscribe"`. `topics_controller.rb`: `tu.save!` on line 212.

**Verification**: The diff confirms both unsubscribe routes are registered as GET. The controller action unconditionally executes `tu.save!` on every request — there is no confirmation step or POST-only guard. The Ember client route renders a confirmation UI but does not perform the mutation; the mutation happens entirely on the server-side GET handler. Any HTTP client that follows the link — email security scanners, prefetch proxies, or Outlook's Safe Links — will permanently alter the user's notification level. This is a concrete, constructible trigger requiring only that an email client follow the unsubscribe link automatically.

**Remediation**: Move the mutation to a POST action; make the GET route render a confirmation page only, and have the template submit a POST form to perform the actual notification-level change.

**Adjacent observations**: The Ember front-end route renders a confirmation UI (`topic/unsubscribe.hbs`) with a notification-level dropdown, suggesting the intended UX was a two-step flow; the server GET handler mutating state on first load contradicts this design intent.

golden_comments: BlockedEmail.should_block_email? method has side effects during a read operation - it updates statistics even when just checking if an email should be blocked. This could cause race conditions in concurrent environments and makes the method name misleading.
Regex pattern @(#{domains}) only matches domain suffixes, not full domains. evil.example.com would match whitelist entry example.com.
candidates:
rejectedEmails initialized as shared Em.A([]) on prototype, not per instance

- **Location**: `app/assets/javascripts/discourse/controllers/create_account_controller.js:17`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist (item 9 — zero-value sentinel / prototype aliasing)

**Mechanism**: `Em.A([])` is evaluated once at class-definition time and assigned as a property value inside the `.extend({...})` literal. In Ember 1.x, non-primitive property values are placed on the prototype and shared across all instances of the class. `pushObject` mutates the shared array in place.

**Consequence**: Every instantiation of `CreateAccountController` (modal re-opens, new tabs using the same page session) sees the accumulated rejected emails from prior sessions. A legitimate email entered after a previous session rejected it is falsely blocked client-side until full page reload.

**Evidence**: `rejectedEmails: Em.A([]),` — line 17 of the new file; `pushObject` call at line ~274.

**Verification**: In Ember 1.x, array/object values defined directly on `.extend({...})` literals land on the prototype, which is a well-documented footgun. `pushObject` mutates rather than reassigns, so every instance observes the shared state. A user who sees "invalid" after one attempt, closes the modal, and reopens it with the same (or any prior-rejected) email will continue to see the cached rejection.

**Remediation**: Initialize inside `init`:
```js
init: function() { this._super(); this.set('rejectedEmails', Em.A([])); }
```

`email_in_restriction_setting?` regex lacks trailing anchor, enabling whitelist bypass on domain-suffix variations

- **Location**: `lib/validators/email_validator.rb:19`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist (item 5 — surface-level fix bypassing core mechanism)

**Mechanism**: `domains = setting.gsub('.', '\.')` escapes dots, then `regexp = Regexp.new("@(#{domains})", true)` builds a pattern with no trailing anchor. The regex matches the first `@<domain>` substring anywhere inside the email.

**Consequence**: For an `email_domains_whitelist` of `example.com`, the email `user@example.com.attacker.tld` contains `@example.com` as a substring, so `value =~ regexp` returns truthy and the whitelist admits an attacker-controlled domain. This is the more dangerous direction; the blacklist side is over-broad (rejects legitimate subdomains by the same logic) but does not break security.

**Evidence**: `regexp = Regexp.new("@(#{domains})", true)` — line 19; no `$` / `\z` anchor.

**Verification**: The regex is unanchored at the tail. Ruby's `=~` returns the match offset, which is truthy, causing `email_in_restriction_setting?` to return a match for whitelisted-string-prefix domains. An attacker registers on a domain whose name contains the whitelisted domain as a left-boundary substring and passes the whitelist gate. The bug was carried forward from the previously inline `email_validator` method.

**Remediation**: Anchor to end-of-string: `Regexp.new("@(#{domains})\\z", true)`.

Client caches all email-error types, so uniqueness-rejected emails display the generic "invalid" message on resubmission

- **Location**: `app/assets/javascripts/discourse/controllers/create_account_controller.js:272`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: structural-target (string-based type discrimination)

**Mechanism**: The success handler pushes `result.values.email` into `rejectedEmails` whenever `result.errors && result.errors.email && result.values` is truthy. `user.errors.to_hash` on the server side emits every email validation error — uniqueness (`"has already been taken"`), presence, format, whitelist/blacklist, and blocked. The client does not discriminate by error category. Once the email is in `rejectedEmails`, `emailValidation` short-circuits with `I18n.t('user.email.invalid')`.

**Consequence**: A user who attempts to register with an already-taken email (the legitimate "you already have an account" case) is told the email is "invalid" on their next keystroke — they cannot tell whether to choose a different address or log in. The same misclassification applies to any future server-side email error, not just blocked emails.

**Evidence**: `if (result.errors && result.errors.email && result.values) { … pushObject(result.values.email) }` — line ~274; `reason: I18n.t('user.email.invalid')` — line ~68.

**Verification**: Trace: user submits `taken@example.com` → server returns `errors: {email: ["has already been taken"]}`, `values: {email: "taken@example.com", ...}` → client pushes `taken@example.com` into `rejectedEmails` → on any character typed, `emailValidation` sees `contains(email)` and returns `{ failed: true, reason: I18n.t('user.email.invalid') }`. The server's actual error message is discarded.

**Remediation**: Either surface the server's real error message (`result.errors.email[0]` or the existing flash) instead of a hard-coded "invalid", or gate the `pushObject` to only the blocked/invalid error categories — e.g., check for the blocked translation key or a server-side `result.errors.email_blocked` flag.

golden_comments: SSRF vulnerability using open(url) without validation
The current origin validation using indexOf is insufficient and can be bypassed. An attacker could use a malicious domain like evil-discourseUrl.com to pass this check.
postMessage targetOrigin should be the origin (scheme+host+port), not the full referrer URL; using the full URL will cause the message to be dropped and prevent resizing.

The code sets X-Frame-Options: ALLOWALL which completely disables clickjacking protection. The referer validation can be bypassed (referer headers are easily spoofed), and the fallback to empty string for nil referer masks validation failures.

The TopicEmbed.import method is susceptible to a NoMethodError if the contents parameter is nil when attempting to append a string, and an XSS vulnerability due to unescaped url interpolation in the generated HTML.
The ERB block closes with end if, which is invalid Ruby/ERB and will raise at render; it should just be end to close the if block.
candidates:
embed.js postMessage origin check uses substring match, allowing spoofed origins

- **Location**: `app/assets/javascripts/embed.js:84-84`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `substring-instead-of-equality origin check`

**Mechanism**: `discourseUrl.indexOf(e.origin) === -1` tests whether e.origin appears as a substring of discourseUrl rather than testing equality.

**Consequence**: Any window whose origin is a substring of discourseUrl can send arbitrary postMessage events to resize the iframe, bypassing the origin trust boundary.

**Evidence**: Line 84: `if (discourseUrl.indexOf(e.origin) === -1) { return; }` — should be `e.origin !== discourseUrl`.

**Verification**: Confirmed in embed.js line 84. The check uses indexOf to test substring containment, not origin equality. A cross-origin window whose full origin string is a substring of discourseUrl (e.g. origin `https://a.b` when discourseUrl is `https://a.b.example.com/`) passes. The check is also directionally wrong — it asks whether e.origin appears in discourseUrl instead of comparing them. Reclassified critical→major: exploitation requires controlling an origin that is a substring of the configured discourse URL.

*Reclassified from behavioral/critical*

**Remediation**: Replace with strict origin equality check.

**Adjacent observations**:
- discourseUrl itself is a JS global set by the embed-host page, so its value is controlled by the third-party embedder, not directly by an attacker.

best.html.erb uses invalid `end if` ERB tag, causing SyntaxError on every render

- **Location**: `app/views/embed/best.html.erb:414-414`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: critical | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist

**Mechanism**: `<%- end if %>` is invalid Ruby — `end` terminates a block and `if` without a condition is a parse error.

**Consequence**: The embed view raises ActionView::Template::Error on every render, breaking the primary embed path.

**Evidence**: Line 414: `<%- end if %>` closes the if/else block — should be `<% end %>`.

**Verification**: The if/else block opens at line 410 `<%- if @topic_view.posts.present? %>`, else at line 412, and tries to close with `<%- end if %>` — the ERB compiler emits `end if`, a Ruby SyntaxError (bare `end` followed by `if` with no condition). Every render raises ActionView::Template::Error. A correctly-formed `<% end %>` appears at line 437 for the second if block, confirming the line 414 form is unintentional.

**Remediation**: Replace `<%- end if %>` with `<% end %>`.

**Adjacent observations**:
- If this view executed, `@topic_view.topic.url` is referenced in the empty-posts branch via the start_discussion link — a topic without posts could still be linked if topic_id was found and TopicView created.

layouts/embed.html.erb injects unescaped request.referer into JS string literal

- **Location**: `app/views/layouts/embed.html.erb:474-474`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `unescaped server value interpolated into JS string`

**Mechanism**: `parent.postMessage({...}, '<%= request.referer %>')` interpolates Referer directly into a single-quoted JS string without escape_javascript.

**Consequence**: A single quote in the Referer terminates the string; attacker-controlled referer can inject JavaScript and redirect postMessage targetOrigin.

**Evidence**: Diff line 474: unescaped `<%= request.referer %>` inside JS string literal.

**Verification**: Line 474 places the Referer header's raw value in a single-quoted JavaScript string. No escape_javascript/j() call wraps the interpolation. Beyond JS injection, the value is passed as targetOrigin to postMessage — attacker-controlled content there can redirect the message to an unintended origin. `ensure_embeddable` enforces the referer's host matches embeddable_host, but the rest of the referer (path, query) is preserved verbatim.

**Remediation**: Use `<%= j(request.referer) %>`.

**Adjacent observations**:
- request.referer is also not HTML-escaped when interpolated into the JS string; if the page were viewed rather than iframed, a `</script>` in the referer would close the script context.

TopicEmbed.absolutize_urls mishandles protocol-relative URLs (//host/path)

- **Location**: `app/models/topic_embed.rb:384-395`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent

**Mechanism**: `href.start_with?('/')` matches `//host/path`; `sub(/^\/+/, '')` strips all leading slashes, producing `prefix/host/path`.

**Consequence**: RSS items with protocol-relative asset URLs have images/links silently rewritten to broken URLs on the source domain.

**Evidence**: Lines 384-395 topic_embed.rb — no guard against `//`.

**Verification**: Traced: for input `//cdn.example.com/img.png`, start_with?('/') is true, sub(/^\/+/, '') yields `cdn.example.com/img.png`, and interpolation produces `https://source.com/cdn.example.com/img.png` — a non-existent path on the source host instead of the intended CDN. Identical logic applies to img src at lines 388-392. Protocol-relative URLs are common in RSS feed HTML.

**Remediation**: Add `!href.start_with?('//')` guard.

Post#cook raw_html short-circuit bypasses Plugin::Filter after_post_cook hook

- **Location**: `app/models/post.rb:309-309`
- **Type**: structural | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `surface-level fix bypassing core mechanism`

**Mechanism**: `return raw if cook_method == Post.cook_methods[:raw_html]` exits `cook` before `Plugin::Filter.apply(:after_post_cook, ...)`.

**Consequence**: Plugin filters registered for `:after_post_cook` (sanitization, spam detection) are silently skipped for imported posts; XSS risk if feed content is attacker-influenced and a plugin would have sanitized it.

**Evidence**: Lines 306-312 post.rb — early return bypasses filter chain.

**Verification**: The early return at line 309 skips the plugin filter chain for raw_html posts. The comment explicitly frames this as "skip the rendering pipeline." No concrete filter in the diff is demonstrably bypassed, so downgrading from behavioral/major to structural/minor: the bypass is an intentional design choice; consequence depends on out-of-diff plugin registrations.

*Reclassified from behavioral/major*

**Remediation**: Run `Plugin::Filter.apply` on raw_html path or sanitize raw before return.

**Adjacent observations**:
- poll_feed.rb runs `CGI.unescapeHTML` + `scrub` on feed content, which does not sanitize HTML — script tags survive untouched into the raw post body.

open-uri used with unvalidated URL in poll_feed and import_remote

- **Location**: `app/jobs/scheduled/poll_feed.rb:275-275` (same pattern at `app/models/topic_embed.rb:368`)
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `unvalidated Kernel#open`

**Mechanism**: `open(SiteSetting.feed_polling_url)` invokes Kernel#open which, with open-uri loaded, reads local files for `/path` and `file://` and (on pre-3.0 Ruby) executes shell for `|cmd`.

**Consequence**: An admin-set `feed_polling_url` of `|cat /etc/passwd` or `/etc/passwd` reads arbitrary files or runs shell commands. TopicEmbed.import_remote at line 368 has the same pattern — its url parameter is constrained by TopicRetriever's host check in the retriever flow, but not in the disqus.thor flow which calls `import_remote` directly.

**Evidence**: Line 275 poll_feed.rb: `SimpleRSS.parse open(SiteSetting.feed_polling_url)`. Line 368 topic_embed.rb: `open(url).read`.

**Verification**: poll_feed.rb has `require 'open-uri'` and calls `open(SiteSetting.feed_polling_url)` with no scheme validation. Kernel#open on this Rails 4 vintage (pre-Ruby 3) accepts pipe-commands and file paths. Admin privilege does not defuse the issue: a compromised admin account or SSRF-adjacent vector converts admin write access to RCE. The import_remote path is partially mitigated by upstream host validation, but poll_feed has none.

**Remediation**: Use `URI.open` after explicit http/https scheme validation.

**Adjacent observations**:
- poll_feed validates each feed item's url with `url !~ /^https?\:\/\//` before calling `TopicEmbed.import`, but that only screens the item URL string used inside the post, not the fetch URL.

golden_comments: Mixing float: left with flexbox causes layout issues. Further this PR removes the float-based right alignment for .d-header .panel, which may cause the login panel in the non-Ember/noscript header (where .panel is nested inside .row and not a flex item) to stack under the title instead of remaining right-aligned.
-ms-align-items never existed in any version of IE/Edge; the correct legacy property is -ms-flex-align.
candidates:
`-ms-align-items` is a fabricated property emitted to all `align-items()` callers

- **Location**: `app/assets/stylesheets/common/foundation/mixins.scss:141`
- **Type**: structural | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: structural-target | **Pattern**: `dead infrastructure`

**Mechanism**: `-ms-align-items: $alignment;` — this property does not exist in any browser vendor spec. Microsoft's IE10/IE11 flexbox implementation used `-ms-flex-align`, which is already emitted on the preceding line. `-ms-align-items` is silently ignored by every browser engine.

**Consequence**: Every call site of `align-items()` emits one extraneous, unrecognized declaration. No browser applies it, so it is dead output. It also creates a false impression of broader IE compatibility coverage than actually exists.

**Evidence**: Line 141 emits `-ms-align-items: $alignment` inside `@mixin align-items`. No Microsoft documentation or CSS specification defines this property. IE10/11 used `-ms-flex-align` (line 140) as the correct vendor-prefixed form.

**Verification**: The diff at line 141 of the added mixin block in mixins.scss clearly shows `-ms-align-items: $alignment;`. The preceding line 140 correctly emits `-ms-flex-align: $alignment;`, which is the actual IE10/11 vendor-prefixed property from the 2011 flexbox draft spec. Microsoft never defined `-ms-align-items` in any published spec or implementation; the property is silently dropped by all browser engines.

**Remediation**: Remove the `-ms-align-items` line; `-ms-flex-align` on line 140 already provides the correct IE10/11 coverage.

**Adjacent observation**: The mixin also emits `-webkit-box-align` and `-webkit-align-items`, which correspond to different drafts of the webkit flexbox spec — this pattern shows the author is aware of multi-draft prefixing, making the fabricated `-ms-align-items` more likely a mistake than intentional.

`align-items` mixin passes values verbatim to `-ms-flex-align`, which uses a different keyword vocabulary

- **Location**: `app/assets/stylesheets/common/foundation/mixins.scss:137-143`
- **Type**: fragile | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: structural-target | **Pattern**: `semantic drift`

**Mechanism**: `-ms-flex-align: $alignment` receives the same string passed to the modern `align-items` property. The IE10 old-spec flexbox property `-ms-flex-align` accepted different keyword values: `start` (not `flex-start`), `end` (not `flex-end`). If any caller passes `flex-start` or `flex-end`, IE10 silently ignores the declaration and falls back to the default (`stretch`), producing wrong vertical alignment.

**Consequence**: A future caller invoking `@include align-items(flex-start)` would get correct rendering in modern browsers but `stretch` alignment in IE10, causing a visual layout regression in that browser. The mixin's cross-browser guarantee is broken for the most commonly used non-center values.

**Evidence**: Line 140: `-ms-flex-align: $alignment` — current callers pass `center` (header.scss, topic-post.scss) and `baseline` (badges.css.scss), which happen to be valid in both old and new specs. A future caller using `flex-start`/`flex-end` would silently fail in IE10.

**Verification**: The 2011 flexbox draft spec (which `-ms-flex-align` belongs to) used keyword values `start`, `end`, `center`, `baseline`, `stretch` — not the modern `flex-start`/`flex-end`. All three actual callers in the diff use `center` or `baseline`, which are safe in both specs. The fragility is real but latent: it requires a caller to pass `flex-start`/`flex-end` to manifest. Reclassified severity from major to minor because the issue only manifests against a future caller input, not against the code in this PR.

*Reclassified from fragile/major.*

**Remediation**: Add value translation logic in the mixin (`flex-start` → `start`, `flex-end` → `end`) before assigning to `-ms-flex-align`, or document that only `center`/`baseline`/`stretch` are supported.

**Adjacent observation**: The `order()` mixin has an analogous vendor-prefix structure but does not share this problem — the 2011 spec used integer values for order, same as the modern spec.

golden_comments: The include_website_name method is missing the required ? suffix. Rails serializers expect include_ methods to end with ? for conditional attribute inclusion, a convention followed by other methods in this serializer. Without it, the website_name attribute may not be conditionally included as intended. Additionally, the '.' << website_host string concatenation should be replaced with '.' + website_host or '.#{website_host}' to avoid mutating string literals, which can lead to issues.
candidates:
String literal mutation via `<<` operator in `ends_with?` check

- **Location**: `app/serializers/user_serializer.rb:155`
- **Type**: fragile | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `string-mutation`

**Mechanism**: `"." << website_host` uses `<<` (shovel) to mutate the left operand. Without `# frozen_string_literal: true`, each evaluation of `"."` produces a fresh String, so the cross-request corruption risk does not materialize on current Ruby. If the file adopts `# frozen_string_literal: true` (widespread default), the line raises FrozenError. In the current code as-is: the computed string is `".#{website_host}"` and the ends_with? check is correct.

**Consequence**: Fragile against a routine pragma change; reviewers must verify literal-freshness invariant to audit.

**Evidence**: `discourse_host.ends_with?("." << website_host)`.

**Verification**: `discourse_host.ends_with?("." << website_host)` appears in the else branch of `website_name`. Without `# frozen_string_literal: true` each call gets a fresh `"."` so the current behavior is correct; adding the pragma would raise FrozenError. Fragile/minor per the type-severity matrix.

*Reclassified from behavioral/major*

**Remediation**: Replace `"." << website_host` with `"." + website_host` or `".#{website_host}"`.

Sibling-subdomain branch (case 2) has no test coverage

- **Location**: `spec/serializers/user_serializer_spec.rb:70-93`
- **Type**: test-integrity | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `composition-opacity`

**Mechanism**: Existing specs cover: different domain, same domain, discourse-is-subdomain-of-website. The middle elsif branch (`same label-count > 2 AND matching tail → host+path; else host`) is not exercised.

**Consequence**: Regressions in the sibling-subdomain branch would not be caught.

**Evidence**: Three `it` blocks; none set up two same-label-count >2-label hostnames with a common root.

**Verification**: The three `it` blocks test discourse.org (else branch), example.com (branch 1), forums.example.com with website example.com (branch 3). No test sets up two same-label-count >2-label hostnames with matching tail to exercise the elsif branch.

**Adjacent observation**: The elsif body contains a ternary with two return values (host+path vs host-only); both sub-cases are untested.

**Remediation**: Add a spec context: `website='http://www.example.com/user'`, `current_hostname='forums.example.com'`, expect `website_name` to eq `'www.example.com/user'`.

Redundant URI parsing — `URI(website.to_s)` called up to four times

- **Location**: `app/serializers/user_serializer.rb:137-150`
- **Type**: structural | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: structural-target | **Pattern**: `caller-reimplementation`

**Mechanism**: `URI(website.to_s)` is parsed 2-3 times across the method body instead of being parsed once into a local variable.

**Consequence**: Minor CPU waste and rescue-scope ambiguity (only the first parse is guarded by `rescue nil`; later `.path` calls rely on the implicit invariant that parsing is deterministic for the same input).

**Evidence**: Multiple `URI(website.to_s)` expressions across the branches.

**Verification**: `URI(website.to_s).host rescue nil` at method top; `URI(website.to_s).path` in branches 1, 2 (ternary), and 3 — up to 4 parse invocations per call. Single local variable removes redundancy and consolidates rescue scope.

**Remediation**: Parse once: `uri = URI(website.to_s) rescue nil; return if uri.nil? || uri.host.nil?` then use `uri.host`/`uri.path`.

`include_website_name` gates on `website.present?` not `website_name.present?`

- **Location**: `app/serializers/user_serializer.rb:156-158`
- **Type**: structural | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `zero-value-sentinel-ambiguity`

**Mechanism**: `include_website_name` returns true whenever `website.present?`. `website_name` can still return nil (URI parse failure, scheme-less URL with no host). In that case the JSON payload includes `website_name: null` instead of omitting the key.

**Consequence**: Minor data-contract inconsistency — the `include_*` convention is supposed to suppress the key when no meaningful value exists.

**Evidence**: `def include_website_name; website.present?; end`.

**Verification**: `website_name` returns Ruby nil via `return if website_host.nil?` when URI.host is nil (e.g., `'not-a-url'`, `'ftp://'`). `include_website_name` only checks `website.present?`, so JSON carries `website_name: null` for such values. The `include_*` convention is meant to suppress the key entirely.

**Remediation**: Change to `def include_website_name; website_name.present?; end` (memoize `website_name` to avoid recompute).

Website-is-subdomain-of-discourse case unhandled — produces host-only instead of host+path

- **Location**: `app/serializers/user_serializer.rb:148-153`
- **Type**: fragile | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `surface-level-fix`

**Mechanism**: Branch 3 checks `discourse_host.ends_with?("." + website_host)` — only covers the case where discourse is a subdomain of the website domain. The inverse (website is subdomain of discourse domain, e.g., website=forum.example.com on instance=example.com) falls through to host-only. Asymmetric treatment of same-organization domains.

**Consequence**: When a user's website is a subdomain of the Discourse instance's root domain, the path is omitted even though the two hosts are conceptually related.

**Evidence**: `website='http://forum.example.com/profile'`, `current_hostname='example.com'`. Branch 3 condition evaluates to false because `'example.com'` does not end with `'.forum.example.com'`.

**Verification**: With `website='http://forum.example.com/profile'`, `current_hostname='example.com'`: branch 1 false (hosts differ), branch 2 false (3 vs 2 labels), branch 3 false (`'example.com'.ends_with?('.forum.example.com')` false) → returns host-only. Asymmetric with the case-3 test.

**Adjacent observation**: Inline comments document three cases but omit the inverse parent-child direction; design intent unclear.

**Remediation**: Add branch: `elsif website_host.ends_with?("." + discourse_host)` returning host+path; or symmetrize the `ends_with` check across both directions.

Light-theme lightness swapped from 30% to 70% in desktop/topic-post.scss reply-to-tab link color

- **Location**: diff line 620 (desktop/topic-post.scss `.reply-to-tab a` inside `.row .topic-body .reply-to-tab`)

Go int64 zero-value serializes as 0 but TypeScript default is undefined — wire contract broken

- **Location**: `packages/grafana-runtime/src/config.ts:97` (also `packages/grafana-data/src/types/config.ts`, `pkg/api/dtos/frontend_settings.go`)
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `zero-sentinel-ambiguity`

**Mechanism**: `anonymousDeviceLimit = undefined` is the TypeScript default and the field is typed `number | undefined`, but the Go DTO field `AnonymousDeviceLimit int64` marshals to JSON `0` when unconfigured (Go zero value). The frontend therefore always receives `0`, never `undefined`.

**Consequence**: Any frontend consumer that branches on `config.anonymousDeviceLimit === undefined` to detect "feature not configured" always gets false. The type annotation promises `undefined` is a possible value from the wire, but no Go code path can produce JSON `null` for an `int64` field.

**Evidence**: `packages/grafana-data/src/types/config.ts` `anonymousDeviceLimit: number | undefined`; `packages/grafana-runtime/src/config.ts` `anonymousDeviceLimit = undefined`; `pkg/api/dtos/frontend_settings.go` `AnonymousDeviceLimit int64`.

**Verification**: Go DTO declares `AnonymousDeviceLimit int64` (non-pointer); the setting uses `MustInt64(0)`. A non-pointer int64 in a Go struct marshals to JSON `0` when zero-valued — `encoding/json` never emits `null` for a non-pointer scalar. The TypeScript type `number | undefined` with default `undefined` creates a false promise that `undefined` can arrive from the wire.

**Remediation**: Use `*int64` in the Go DTO so unset marshals to JSON `null`, or change TS type to `number` with 0 meaning unlimited and drop the `| undefined` branch.

**Adjacent**: Fix requires coordinated change across Go DTO (`*int64`) and TS type, or standardizing on 0 as the unlimited sentinel and dropping `undefined` from the TS type.

updateDevice returns ErrDeviceLimitReached when existing device is outside 30-day window, conflating expired-device with new-device cases

- **Location**: `pkg/services/anonymous/anonimpl/anonstore/database.go:108`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `semantic-drift`

**Mechanism**: `updateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached` when `rowsAffected == 0`. Zero rows are affected both when the `device_id` does not exist (new device, correct to block) and when the `device_id` exists but `updated_at` is outside `[device.UpdatedAt-30d, device.UpdatedAt+1min]` (expired row). A returning user whose device record is older than 30 days receives the same error as a brand-new device.

**Consequence**: When count ≥ limit and a returning user's prior device record aged past 30 days, `updateDevice` returns `ErrDeviceLimitReached`, `Authenticate` aborts (`return nil, err`), and the user cannot authenticate anonymously even though their device cookie references a valid row.

**Evidence**: `if rowsAffected == 0 { return ErrDeviceLimitReached }` in `updateDevice`; `WHERE updated_at BETWEEN device.UpdatedAt-30d AND device.UpdatedAt+1min` excludes existing rows whose `updated_at` falls outside that window.

**Verification**: `updateDevice` WHERE clause is `device_id = ? AND updated_at BETWEEN ? AND ?`; if a row for the given `device_id` exists but its `updated_at` is outside the 30-day window, `rowsAffected=0` and `ErrDeviceLimitReached` is returned. `client.go`'s `Authenticate` treats `ErrDeviceLimitReached` as a hard block (`return nil, err`). Constructible: limit=10, 10 active devices, a user whose record is 31 days old refreshes — `Authenticate` aborts with "device limit reached" even though their device row exists.

**Remediation**: Probe `device_id` existence separately from the time-window check and return a distinct error (e.g., `ErrDeviceExpired`) for stale records; the caller can then decide whether to attempt reinsertion.

**Adjacent**: `CountDevices` gate uses the same time window, so stale rows are excluded from the count — meaning the limit appears "full" with recent devices even when stale records exist. A user whose record aged past 30 days cannot refresh it while the recent-device count is at limit, creating a self-perpetuating lockout until a recent device ages out naturally.

permDenialCache and permCache share backing store with key-space overlap enabling cross-type collisions

- **Location**: `pkg/services/authz/rbac/cache.go:66-68`
- **Type**: fragile | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `key-space collision`

**Mechanism**: `userPermCacheKey` returns `namespace + ".perm_" + userUID + "_" + action`; `userPermDenialCacheKey` returns `namespace + ".perm_" + userUID + "_" + action + "_" + name + "_" + parent`. Both `permCache` and `permDenialCache` are `newCacheWrap` instances backed by the same underlying `cache.LocalCache` instance (constructed once in `NewService`/`setupService`). A denial key for `action="dashboards:read", name="dash1", parent=""` equals the key for a permission entry with `action="dashboards:read_dash1_"`. If a future action string contains underscores combined with specific name/parent values, a denial-cache write can overwrite a serialized `map[string]bool` with a serialized `bool` at the same backing-store key.

**Consequence**: A serialized `bool` written by `permDenialCache.Set` can occupy the same backing-store key as a `permCache` entry. The next `permCache.Get` will fail to deserialize a `bool` as `map[string]bool`, triggering a cache miss that forces `Check`/`List` for that action to hit the DB, degrading performance.

**Evidence**: `cache.go` line 63: `namespace + ".perm_" + userUID + "_" + action`; `cache.go` line 67: `namespace + ".perm_" + userUID + "_" + action + "_" + name + "_" + parent`; `service.go` `NewService` and `setupService` both construct `permCache` and `permDenialCache` from the same `cache` parameter.

**Verification**: Both caches receive the same `cache` parameter in `NewService`; same pattern in `setupService`. Key functions share the `.perm_` prefix with no type discriminator. Concrete collision: `userPermCacheKey(ns, uid, "dashboards:read_dash1_")` == `userPermDenialCacheKey(ns, uid, "dashboards:read", "dash1", "")` both produce `ns.perm_uid_dashboards:read_dash1_`. Real Grafana actions use colons not underscores, so the specific collision is implausible today but structurally real. Severity downgraded to minor: no real action currently triggers it, and the consequence is performance degradation (cache miss → DB hit), not data loss or security bypass.

*Reclassified from fragile/major*

**Remediation**: Use a distinct key prefix for denial-cache entries, e.g. `.perm_denial_`, to guarantee the two namespaces cannot overlap.

Async goroutines derive from the cancellable request context, voiding the 10s legacy timeout

- **Location**: `pkg/apiserver/rest/dualwriter_mode3.go:113-120` (and Update 230, Delete 187, DeleteCollection 272)
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist (checklist item 10 — context bypass, inverse direction)

**Mechanism**: Each async goroutine derives its context via `context.WithTimeoutCause(ctx, time.Second*10, ...)` where `ctx` is the HTTP request context captured from the enclosing handler. In the Kubernetes apiserver, the request context is cancelled when the ResponseWriter is closed after the handler returns — which happens immediately after the goroutine is launched and the handler's `return` statement runs.

**Consequence**: The async legacy write fails with `context canceled` right after the HTTP response is sent. Legacy storage is never actually updated. The 10-second timeout provides no protection and no actual async behaviour occurs — legacy writes silently error out on every request. This defeats the stated intent of mode 3 (dual write to legacy).

**Verification**: All four goroutines (Create line 114, Delete line 187, Update line 230, DeleteCollection line 272) use `context.WithTimeoutCause(ctx, time.Second*10, ...)` where `ctx` is the outer handler's request context captured by closure. `WithTimeoutCause` creates a child of `ctx`; cancellation propagates immediately to the child, causing `d.Legacy.*` calls inside the goroutine to receive cancellation right after the handler returns — before the 10-second timeout fires. The 10s timeout is effectively meaningless.

**Remediation**: Use `context.WithoutCancel(ctx)` (Go 1.21+) as the base before applying the 10s timeout, so the goroutine outlives the HTTP response. Alternatively, derive from `context.Background()` with manual propagation of trace/log keys.

*Adjacent: `context.WithoutCancel` was introduced in Go 1.21; check toolchain version before applying.*

golden_comments: The GetWebAssets function implements an incomplete double-checked locking pattern for caching web assets. The function first checks if the cache is populated using a read lock (RLock), and if the cache is empty, it acquires a write lock to populate it. However, it fails to re-check whether the cache was populated by another goroutine while waiting to acquire the write lock.

In addition to the missing double-check, the function has a critical flaw in its error handling: it unconditionally assigns the fetch result to the cache (line 69: entryPointAssetsCache = result) regardless of whether the fetch succeeded or failed. When an error occurs during asset fetching, result is nil, and this nil value overwrites any previously valid cache entry.

Missing double-checked locking — cache not rechecked after write-lock acquisition

- **Location**: `pkg/api/webassets/webassets.go:44-50`
- **Type**: structural | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `double-checked-locking-omission`
- **Reclassified from**: behavioral/major → structural/minor (no wrong output, only redundant work)

**Mechanism**: `GetWebAssets` reads `entryPointAssetsCache` under RLock, releases it, then unconditionally acquires the write lock without rechecking the cache. If N goroutines all observe `ret == nil` under RLock, all N queue on the write lock and each recomputes `result` and overwrites `entryPointAssetsCache`.

**Consequence**: Under concurrent cold-start load, every goroutine that observed nil under RLock performs a full asset-manifest load serialized under the write lock. Work is redundantly repeated proportional to queue depth instead of executed once as the cache pattern intends. Functional output remains correct — the finding is about wasted I/O and serialization, not wrong results.

**Evidence**: Lines 44–50: RLock taken, `ret := entryPointAssetsCache` read, RUnlock, `if cfg.Env != setting.Dev && ret != nil { return ret, nil }`, then `Lock()` — no second read of `entryPointAssetsCache` between acquiring the write lock and proceeding.

**Verification**: Tracing the diff directly: (1) RLock is acquired, pointer copied into `ret`, RUnlock called. (2) If `ret == nil` (or `cfg.Env == Dev`) execution falls through to `Lock()`. (3) No re-check of `entryPointAssetsCache` occurs between write-lock acquisition and computing `result`. N goroutines that all saw `ret == nil` will each perform a full manifest load and overwrite. Each caller still returns an equivalent pointer and the last writer overwrites with an equivalent value — no wrong output, data loss, crash, or security bypass. Reclassified from behavioral/major because redundant work is not wrong output, and no constructible test demonstrates incorrect output.

**Remediation**: After acquiring the write lock, re-read the cache before computing:

```go
entryPointAssetsCacheMu.Lock()
defer entryPointAssetsCacheMu.Unlock()
if cfg.Env != setting.Dev && entryPointAssetsCache != nil {
    return entryPointAssetsCache, nil
}
// ... existing load path
```

**Adjacent observations** (from Challenger):
- In `cfg.Env == setting.Dev` mode the early-return is never taken regardless of cache state, so every Dev request reacquires the write lock and reloads the manifest. Presumably intentional (hot-reload for development) but the write lock is held for the full duration of the manifest load on every Dev request — concurrent Dev requests now serialize where they did not before the diff.
- The transition from a single nil-initialized var to a mutex-guarded var correctly eliminates the pre-existing data race on `entryPointAssetsCache`, which is the primary intent of this diff.

- **Location**: `pkg/storage/unified/search/bleve.go:85-143`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `race on mutable shared cache without dedup guard`

**Consequence**: Under concurrent index construction for the same namespace+resource key — plausible during startup when multiple goroutines process different events for the same resource — one or both indexes diverge from the authoritative state, producing stale or split search results.

The PR's stated intent is reducing lock contention, which confirms concurrent calls are expected. Two concurrent callers for the same key will each build a full bleve index, then both write to the same cache slot — the first write is overwritten and its index handle is orphaned while the second write lands in the cache.

The P-256 DER signature has R,S of at most 32 bytes each, so `asn1derToConcatenatedRS(sign, 96)` zero-pads to 48 bytes. **Remediation**: Generate algorithm-specific key pairs: use `ECGenParameterSpec("secp384r1")` for ES384 and `ECGenParameterSpec("secp521r1")` for ES512.

- **Location**: `crypto/default/src/main/java/org/keycloak/crypto/def/DefaultCryptoProvider.java:769` (also affects `crypto/fips1402/.../FIPS1402Provider.java` and `crypto/elytron/.../WildFlyElytronProvider.java`)

- **Detection source**: structural-target (Parallel collection coupling / undefined ordering)

**Mechanism**: All three production `CryptoProvider` implementations (`DefaultCryptoProvider`, `FIPS1402Provider`, `WildFlyElytronProvider`) return `order()=200`. `CryptoIntegration.detectProvider` sorts by `order()` descending and takes index 0. **Consequence**: On a classpath containing both `crypto/default` and `crypto/fips1402` JARs, the cryptographic provider selected is determined by classloader iteration order, which is undefined. Java's sort is stable, preserving insertion order for equal elements. `ServiceLoader` iteration order across JARs is defined only as classpath order, which is JVM/classloader-dependent. These modules are mutually exclusive deployment choices, but the `fragile/minor` classification correctly captures the undefined behavior when equal-order providers are loaded simultaneously.

**Remediation**: Assign distinct order values (e.g., `FIPS1402Provider=300`, `DefaultCryptoProvider=200`, `WildFlyElytronProvider=150`) so selection is deterministic, or document the tie-break behavior explicitly.

ASN1Decoder.readSequence does not verify inner TLV sum equals declared SEQUENCE length

- **Location**: `authz/client/src/main/java/org/keycloak/authorization/client/util/crypto/ASN1Decoder.java:105`
- **Type**: fragile | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist (Silent error discard)

**Mechanism**: In `readSequence()`, `length` is the declared SEQUENCE content length from `readLength()`. Each `readNext()` call returns the full TLV bytes of an inner element. `length -= bytes.length` decrements by bytes consumed. The loop `while (length > 0)` does not verify `bytes.length <= length` before the decrement and does not check that `length == 0` after exit. Malformed DER where declared SEQUENCE length disagrees with the sum of inner TLV sizes causes the loop to exit either early (leaving trailing bytes) or past the boundary (consuming bytes from outside the sequence) without raising an error.

**Consequence**: Malformed DER signatures produce silently-truncated or over-read results rather than a clear `IOException`. The `seq.size() != 2` check in `asn1derToConcatenatedRS` catches wrong-count cases, so downstream `Signature.verify()` still rejects invalid signatures — but the decoder is less strict than DER requires and could mask bugs in callers that use it for non-ECDSA-signature DER.

**Evidence**: Lines 105-109: `while (length > 0) { byte[] bytes = readNext(); result.add(bytes); length = length - bytes.length; }` — no assertion that `bytes.length <= length` before decrement, no check that `length == 0` after loop exit.

**Verification**: Tracing `readNext()`: `mark()` sets `count=0`, reads tag and length, `reset()` returns `count` and resets the stream. `length += reset()` computes total TLV size. `read(length)` reads the entire TLV, so `bytes.length` equals the inner TLV's full wire size. For malformed DER where the last inner element's wire size exceeds remaining declared length, `length - bytes.length` goes negative, and `while (length > 0)` exits without error. The `seq.size() != 2` guard in `asn1derToConcatenatedRS` (line 492) mitigates attacks on ECDSA signatures, but the decoder itself is insufficiently strict.

**Remediation**: Add `if (bytes.length > length) throw new IOException(...)` before the decrement, and `if (length != 0) throw new IOException(...)` after the loop.

**Adjacent observations**:
- The indefinite-length encoding case (`readLength` returns `-1` when length byte is `0x80`) would cause `readSequence`'s `while (length > 0)` loop to terminate immediately without reading any elements — a separate silent failure mode.
- `readNext()` does not guard against `readLength()` returning `-1` either, so `length += reset()` could compute a malformed read length.

Django QuerySet negative indexing raises AssertionError when `enable_advanced_features` and `cursor.offset < 0`

- **Location**: `src/sentry/api/paginator.py:866-870`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: critical | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist | **Pattern**: `negative-offset-path`

**Mechanism**: In `OptimizedCursorPaginator.get_result`, the branch `if self.enable_advanced_features and cursor.offset < 0:` assigns `start_offset = cursor.offset` (negative) and then executes `list(queryset[start_offset:stop])`. Django QuerySets do not support negative indexing — they raise `AssertionError: Negative indexing is not supported.` unconditionally. The inline comment "The underlying Django ORM properly handles negative slicing automatically" is factually wrong.

**Consequence**: Any superuser or org member with `has_global_access` who sends `?optimized_pagination=true` and crafts a cursor with a negative offset value will receive a 500 error. More importantly, this is a deliberately introduced broken code path on an audit-log endpoint whose comments frame it as safe.

**Evidence**: Cursor offset is user-controlled (base64-encoded in the `cursor` query parameter). `Cursor.__init__` only applies `int(offset)` coercion — no lower-bound validation. A crafted cursor with `offset=-1` routes into the negative branch and triggers `AssertionError` on any non-trivial queryset.

**Verification**: The diff's branch unconditionally attempts negative slicing against a Django QuerySet; the documented framework behavior is AssertionError. The comments normalize and legitimize a pattern that is guaranteed to fail.

**Remediation**: Remove the negative-offset branch entirely; Django ORM does not support negative slicing. If reverse pagination is desired, use `is_prev` cursor semantics with a non-negative offset, matching existing cursor conventions.

**Adjacent**: The comment falsely claims Django ORM handles negative slicing automatically — this contradicts documented framework behavior and is itself a comment-code-drift finding (see retrospective).

**Mechanism**: `if 'event.type:error' in query:` performs an unanchored substring match on the lowercased query string. A negated query `!event.type:error` contains the substring `event.type:error` starting at index 1. A prefix-form such as `event.type:errorcode` also matches spuriously.

**Consequence**: Upsampling activates for queries that are not error-focused (e.g. a user-constructed negation filter like `!event.type:error` intended to exclude errors). `upsampled_count()` will aggregate `sum(sample_weight)` over a result set that does not represent error events, producing incorrect counts.

**Verification**: Line 263 contains the literal substring check. Character-level inspection confirms `!event.type:error` contains the full substring at offset 1. The original sighting also claimed list-form `event.type:[error,transaction]` would match, but after `event.type:` the next character is `[`, so the substring check fails for list form — this case was corrected during verification.

**Adjacent observations**: The issue is string-based type discrimination across an unsanitised query DSL — the correct approach is to parse the query filter tree, not grep the raw string.

**Remediation**: Parse the query using Sentry's existing search-filter machinery and check the parsed `event.type` filter explicitly, or use a regex with boundaries rejecting negations and prefix forms.

safe_urlopen call outside try block can propagate unhandled exceptions

- **Location**: `src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py:455-461`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist (item 14)

**Mechanism**: `safe_urlopen(url=ghip.get_oauth_access_token_url(), data=data)` is called OUTSIDE the try/except block in `OAuthLoginView.dispatch`. If `safe_urlopen` raises (network failure, DNS error, timeout, SSL error), the exception propagates beyond `OAuthLoginView.dispatch`. Only `safe_urlread` is protected by the `try/except Exception` block.

**Consequence**: If `safe_urlopen` raises, the exception propagates and produces an unhandled 500 error instead of the graceful `error()` response. Users see a 500 page during network transients to GitHub's token endpoint, rather than the controlled error template other failure modes in the same method emit.

**Evidence**: In the diff, `req = safe_urlopen(url=ghip.get_oauth_access_token_url(), data=data)` is outside the `try:` block. The structure is:
```python
req = safe_urlopen(url=ghip.get_oauth_access_token_url(), data=data)

try:
    body = safe_urlread(req).decode("utf-8")
    payload = dict(parse_qsl(body))
except Exception:
    payload = {}
```
Only `safe_urlread` and `parse_qsl` are protected.

**Verification**: At diff line 455, `req = safe_urlopen(...)` appears before the `try:` at line 457. The try/except block only wraps `safe_urlread(req)` and the body parsing (lines 458-461). Any exception raised by `safe_urlopen` propagates out of `OAuthLoginView.dispatch` without being caught, resulting in an unhandled 500 rather than the graceful `error(request, self.active_organization)` response the method uses for all other failure modes.

**Remediation**: Move `safe_urlopen` inside the try block so network errors produce the graceful `error()` response:
```python
try:
    req = safe_urlopen(url=ghip.get_oauth_access_token_url(), data=data)
    body = safe_urlread(req).decode("utf-8")
    payload = dict(parse_qsl(body))
except Exception:
    payload = {}
```

**Mechanism**: The guard is `if self.process_restarts[process_index] > MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS: raise RuntimeError(...)`. The increment happens after. With `MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS=N`, a process can crash N+1 times before the consumer is restarted because restart count N is not `> N`.

**Consequence**: A flusher process can crash one more time than `MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS` before the consumer is restarted.

**Evidence**: Line 294: `if self.process_restarts[process_index] > MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS:` and line 298: `self.process_restarts[process_index] += 1`. Inherited shape from the pre-diff code.

**Verification**: State trace — crash #1 count=0, 0>N False, increment to 1; … crash #(N+1) count=N, N>N False, increment to N+1; crash #(N+2) count=N+1, N+1>N True, raise. The consumer tolerates N+1 restarts instead of N. `>` should be `>=`.

**Remediation**: Change guard to `>= MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS`.

**Adjacent observation**: The pre-diff code had the same off-by-one on a plain int (`self.process_restarts > MAX_PROCESS_RESTARTS`); the refactor to a per-process dict preserved the bug.

join() passes full timeout to next_step.join then offers zero time to processes

- **Location**: `src/sentry/spans/consumers/process/flusher.py:354-378`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `resource-budget-exhaustion`

**Mechanism**: `self.next_step.join(timeout)` is called with the full original timeout before the per-process loop begins. If `next_step.join` consumes the budget, `deadline - time.time()` is ≤ 0 on the first iteration; the `if remaining_time <= 0: break` guard exits the for-loop entirely, so neither the per-process wait nor `process.terminate()` runs for any remaining process.

**Consequence**: When `next_step.join` consumes the timeout, flusher processes are abandoned (no drain time, no `terminate()` call), risking in-flight message loss and leaked processes on graceful shutdown.

**Evidence**: Lines 361-378.

**Verification**: The `deadline` is computed before `next_step.join(timeout)` which receives the full timeout. On return, the per-process loop checks `remaining_time = deadline - time.time()`; if ≤ 0, `break` exits the for-body before reaching `process.terminate()`. Practical consequence is abandoned (not terminated) processes — loss of drain window and possible resource leak.

**Remediation**: Compute deadline before calling `next_step.join`, or split the timeout between `next_step.join` and the per-process wait.

**Adjacent observation**: When the break fires on iteration k>0, earlier processes have been terminated but later processes are neither waited nor terminated — asymmetric handling across the per-process dict.

If shards are non-empty, `0 or len(shards)` is truthy so `num_processes ≥ 1`. No constructible input triggers the crash.

candidates:
_commit_loop emits no final commit after shutdown_event is set, losing completed offsets

- **Location**: `src/sentry/remote_subscriptions/consumers/queue_consumer.py:301-319`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent | **Pattern**: `silent error discard`

**Mechanism**: `_commit_loop` exits when `shutdown_event` is set. `SimpleQueueProcessingStrategy.close()` sets the event, joins the commit thread, then calls `queue_pool.shutdown()` which drains and joins workers. Offsets completed during the drain window (after the commit thread has joined) are never flushed.

**Consequence**: On graceful shutdown, offsets completed by workers after the commit thread's final iteration are silently dropped; consumer restarts from an earlier offset, causing duplicate processing of the tail of every shutdown.

**Evidence**: `_commit_loop` exits when `shutdown_event.is_set()` (line 302); `SimpleQueueProcessingStrategy.close()` sets the event, joins `commit_thread`, then calls `queue_pool.shutdown()` which joins workers.

**Verification**: `close()` at diff lines 363-366 sets the event, joins the commit thread, then calls `queue_pool.shutdown()`. After `commit_thread.join()` returns, the commit thread is dead. `queue_pool.shutdown()` then calls `q.shutdown(immediate=False)` which allows workers to drain queued items. Any offset completed by a worker during this drain window is recorded via `complete_offset()`, but `get_committable_offsets()` is never called again. Concrete failing sequence: a worker processes an item during the drain, calls `complete_offset(partition, offset)`, then exits — the commit for that offset is never emitted upstream.

**Remediation**: After `commit_thread.join()` (or after `queue_pool.shutdown()` returns), call `get_committable_offsets()` and `commit_function()` one final time before returning from `close()`.

**Adjacent observation**: The loop structure `shutdown_event.wait(1.0)` before committing means that offsets already completed at the moment of shutdown ARE committed in the final iteration triggered by `wait()` returning early. Only offsets completed during the subsequent drain are lost.

MetricAlertDetectorHandler fails to implement abstract method build_occurrence_and_event_data, causing TypeError on instantiation

- **Location**: `src/sentry/incidents/grouptype.py:21-22`
- **Type**: behavioral | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: intent

**Mechanism**: class MetricAlertDetectorHandler(StatefulDetectorHandler[QuerySubscriptionUpdate]): pass inherits build_occurrence_and_event_data which is declared @abc.abstractmethod on StatefulDetectorHandler. The subclass provides no implementation. Python raises TypeError on instantiation.

**Consequence**: Any code path that resolves a detector whose group type uses MetricAlertDetectorHandler will raise TypeError on instantiation - the handler can never be called.

**Evidence**: StatefulDetectorHandler.build_occurrence_and_event_data is decorated @abc.abstractmethod. MetricAlertDetectorHandler provides only `pass` with no override.

**Verification**: The diff at src/sentry/workflow_engine/processors/detector.py lines 123-127 introduces @abc.abstractmethod on build_occurrence_and_event_data. The diff at src/sentry/incidents/grouptype.py lines 21-22 shows MetricAlertDetectorHandler(StatefulDetectorHandler[QuerySubscriptionUpdate]): pass with no override. The detector_handler property in models/detector.py instantiates the handler via group_type.detector_handler(self), which would raise TypeError. However, the comment 'Example GroupType... We don't create these issues yet' suggests no production caller currently exercises this path.

*Reclassified from behavioral/critical*

**Remediation**: Add concrete build_occurrence_and_event_data implementation or remove the StatefulDetectorHandler upgrade until ready.

build_mock_occurrence_and_event ignores the value parameter so tests cannot detect wrong value forwarding

- **Location**: `tests/sentry/workflow_engine/processors/test_detector.py:458-489`
- **Type**: test-integrity | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist

**Mechanism**: build_mock_occurrence_and_event(handler, group_key, value, new_status) accepts value: int but never references it when constructing the IssueOccurrence or event_data. The occurrence is identical regardless of what value is passed.

**Consequence**: Production code passes value to build_occurrence_and_event_data, but the test helper never uses it to influence the expected occurrence. If production code passes wrong value, tests still pass.

**Evidence**: Function signature includes value: int parameter; the body constructs IssueOccurrence without referencing value anywhere.

**Verification**: Lines 458-489 of test_detector.py show build_mock_occurrence_and_event with value: int parameter. Body builds IssueOccurrence with hardcoded id/event_id, uses handler.build_fingerprint(group_key) and new_status.value for initial_issue_priority, but never references value anywhere.

**Remediation**: Use the value parameter in the built occurrence (e.g., store in evidence_data) so tests fail if wrong value forwarded.

PriorityLevel(new_status) silently mis-converts when DetectorPriorityLevel and PriorityLevel integer values diverge

- **Location**: `src/sentry/workflow_engine/processors/detector.py:176-177`
- **Type**: fragile | **Severity**: major | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: checklist

**Mechanism**: PriorityLevel(new_status) converts a DetectorPriorityLevel enum to PriorityLevel by numeric value. If the two enums diverge in integer assignments, wrong member is returned or ValueError is raised.

**Consequence**: Occurrences for non-OK detections would carry wrong initial_issue_priority or ValueError aborts the pipeline. No alignment check exists.

**Evidence**: PriorityLevel(new_status) cast where new_status is typed as DetectorPriorityLevel - separate enum from PriorityLevel.

**Verification**: Diff lines 174-177 show result, event_data = self.build_occurrence_and_event_data(group_key, value, PriorityLevel(new_status)) inside the else branch. new_status is DetectorPriorityLevel (confirmed by surrounding line 286 `new_status != DetectorPriorityLevel.OK`). PriorityLevel(new_status) is a numeric-value-based enum conversion with no alignment guard.

**Remediation**: Add explicit DETECTOR_PRIORITY_TO_PRIORITY mapping dict instead of bare PriorityLevel(new_status) cast.

detector.detector_handler returns a fresh handler instance per access making test fingerprint comparisons identity-fragile

- **Location**: `tests/sentry/workflow_engine/processors/test_detector.py:348-350`
- **Type**: test-integrity | **Severity**: minor | **Origin**: introduced
- **Detection source**: structural-target

**Mechanism**: detector.detector_handler is a property that calls registry.get_by_slug and instantiates a new handler each access. The test calls it twice (once via process_detectors, once via build_mock_occurrence_and_event), getting two different instances.

**Consequence**: Test may pass coincidentally if fingerprint computation is stateless but silently breaks once handler state affects fingerprint. Composition opacity.

**Evidence**: build_mock_occurrence_and_event(detector.detector_handler, ...) called after process_detectors already used detector.detector_handler internally.

**Verification**: Diff for models/detector.py lines 53-56 confirms detector_handler is a @property that constructs a new handler instance on every access. test_state_results calls process_detectors(data_packet, [detector]) then build_mock_occurrence_and_event(detector.detector_handler, ...) - producing two independent instances. build_fingerprint body is unchanged in this diff so instance state usage cannot be fully verified from the diff alone.

**Remediation**: Capture handler instance once and pass it to both call paths.
